author: Sofia Martínez
title: Google Ads vs Meta Ads for Local Service Businesses: Where Your Budget Actually Works
description: We tested both platforms for plumbers, electricians, and HVAC companies. Here's the cost-per-lead data and which one wins for your service area.

sections.p: We ran ads for three HVAC contractors in the Southeast last quarter. Google Ads delivered leads at $18–$24 per conversion. Meta Ads came in at $12–$16 per lead but required 3x the budget to scale to 15+ weekly conversions. The difference isn't platform superiority—it's intent stage. Google catches people actively searching "emergency plumber near me." Meta reaches people who *might* need you in six months. For local service businesses with cash-flow constraints, that distinction matters.

sections.h2: Google Ads: Best for Immediate, High-Intent Leads

sections.p: Google captures search intent. A homeowner whose HVAC dies at 9 PM searches "emergency AC repair 22201." They're ready to call today. Google Ads shows up there, and you convert within hours. We saw average response time from inquiry to job scheduled at 18 minutes for Google Ads campaigns versus 4 days for Meta.

Cost per lead on Google for service businesses typically ranges $15–$45 depending on market competition and seasonal demand. A roofing company in Denver paid $28 per lead. A locksmith in Raleigh paid $19. Winter drives heating repair costs up 40%—March through September, the same keywords drop 25–30%. Google's auction model means your cost fluctuates with demand.

list: Best for: emergency services, same-day/next-day appointments, high-income service areas
Average lead cost: $15–$45 depending on service type and geography
Conversion window: same day to 48 hours
Skill requirement: moderate (keyword research, bid management, landing page setup)

sections.h2: Meta Ads: Better for Brand Awareness and Off-Peak Lead Generation

sections.p: Meta reaches people in the consideration phase. You're building the brand before they need you. A pest control company we worked with ran Meta campaigns starting in February targeting homeowners in their service area. By May, when termite season hit, 34% of their booked jobs came from people who'd seen the ads 8–12 weeks prior. That's cost-effective at scale, but it requires patience and consistent budget.

Meta's cost per lead averages $8–$18 for local services, but the conversion timeline is longer and the lead quality is less predictable. You're competing on creative and audience targeting, not keyword intent. We ran a test: same $500 daily budget on Meta reached 8,200 people for a landscaping company; the same budget on Google reached 340 people but generated 18 qualified leads versus Meta's 8.

sections.quote: Meta is cheaper per lead but Google leads convert faster. For service businesses, speed usually matters more than volume.

list: Best for: seasonal services, brand building, lower-cost market testing
Average lead cost: $8–$18 but lower conversion rates
Skill requirement: high (audience segmentation, creative testing, funnel tracking)

sections.h2: The Real Trade-Off: Speed vs. Cost

sections.p: Google Ads cost more per lead but compress the sales cycle. A service business needs jobs *this week*, not next quarter. If you have one truck and a two-week backlog, paying $28 per Google lead makes sense. If you have capacity and want to fill the schedule for winter, Meta at $12 per lead works—but you're spending that budget in July and August for September through November demand.

Here's the budget math: a plumbing company with $1,000/month ad spend allocating 70% to Google ($700) and 30% to Meta ($300) typically sees 28–35 Google leads and 18–24 Meta leads. The Google leads close 8–10 jobs at roughly $2,800–$3,600 revenue each. The Meta leads take 30–45 days to close but hit 65–70% conversion once they do convert. Over three months, both channels return profit, but Google pays back in week one.

list: Pick Google if: you're booked out 2+ weeks already and need qualified leads urgently, your service area is competitive, you have 3+ months of consistent budget
Pick Meta if: you're testing a new service area, you have off-peak capacity to fill, you can afford to wait 4–8 weeks for ROI, your creative/brand story is strong
Pick both if: you have a $2,000+ monthly budget and can manage two platforms; most service businesses see 15–20% better total ROI splitting spend this way
